    FinServ context: UK financial firms must notify the FCA and ICO within strict timeframes following a breach — delays can result in significant fines on top of any reputational damage from the incident itself.
    First seen: 28 April 2026
    Data Encrypted for Impact
    A ransomware technique where attackers encrypt files and systems to disrupt operations and demand payment for decryption.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions face severe regulatory and customer impact when transaction systems or customer databases are encrypted, often forcing rapid ransom payment decisions.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Data Execution Prevention (DEP)
    A vulnerability where data written to a buffer exceeds its allocated memory space on the program's call stack, potentially allowing code execution.
    FinServ context: Trading applications with buffer overflow vulnerabilities could be exploited to manipulate transaction data or gain unauthorized access to financial systems.
    First seen: 13 April 2026
    Data Exfiltration
    The unauthorised copying or transfer of
    Data Leak (AI Context)
    The unintended exposure of sensitive information caused by an AI agent or model being manip
    Data Silo
    A situation where data is held and used by one part of an organisation but is not accessible to or integrated with other parts, limiting the overall picture available for decision-making.
    FinServ context: In financial institutions, data silos between fraud, identity, and transaction teams prevent a complete customer risk view, weakening both fraud detection and regulatory compliance.
    First seen: 18 April 2026
    DCMTK
    An open-source software toolkit for handling DICOM (medical imaging) data and communications.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms processing healthcare payments or insurance claims may encounter DCMTK vulnerabilities in medical data systems they interface with.
    First seen: 07 April 2026
    DDoS-for-Hire (Booter/Stresser)
    A commercial service that sells distributed denial-of-service attack capability to paying customers, allowing even non-technical criminals to overwhelm and take down internet-facing systems.
    FinServ context: UK financial services firms are high-value targets for DDoS-for-hire attacks, which can disrupt payment platforms, online banking, and trading infrastructure during peak business hours.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    Deep Learning Frameworks
    Software libraries and tools that provide the building blocks for creating and training artificial intelligence models.
    FinServ context: Investment firms use deep learning frameworks like PyTorch to build algorithmic trading models that can analyse market patterns and execute trades automatically.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    Deepfakes
    AI-generated synthetic media that convincingly replaces a person's appearance or voice with someone else's.
    FinServ context: Financial criminals could use deepfakes to impersonate bank executives in video calls to authorize fraudulent wire transfers or bypass voice authentication systems.
    First seen: 06 April 2026
    DeFi (Decentralised Finance)
    A system of financial services built on blockchain technology that operates without traditional intermediaries like banks, using automated smart contracts instead.
    FinServ context: UK financial regulators are increasingly monitoring DeFi platforms because their lack of central oversight creates significant fraud and theft risks, as demonstrated by the $290 million KelpDAO heist.
    First seen: 21 April 2026
    Dependency Chain Attack
    An attack that targets third-party software libraries or packages that a legitimate application relies on, rather than the application itself.
    FinServ context: AI-enabled systems in financial services that use frameworks like PyTorch or LangChain inherit risk from their entire dependency chain, meaning a compromised open-source package could silently affect a trading model or fraud detection system.
    First seen: 15 April 2026
    Deserialization
    The process of converting stored data back into its original object format that a program can use.
    FinServ context: Payment processing systems often deserialize transaction data, making them vulnerable if malicious data is crafted to execute harmful code during this conversion.
    First seen: 30 March 2026
    Destination Buffer
    A specific sequence of network packets or traffic patterns that triggers malware to activate or execute commands.
    FinServ context: Attackers could plant backdoors in financial networks that remain dormant until activated by a secret knock, allowing them to bypass monitoring systems during off-hours trading.
    First seen: 07 April 2026
    detailPlus Endpoint
    A specific API endpoint in PowerJob software that processes database queries but contains a security vulnerability allowing SQL injection attacks.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms using PowerJob for job scheduling could face data breaches if attackers exploit this endpoint to extract sensitive customer or transaction data.
    First seen: 08 April 2026
    Determine_current_user filter
    A WordPress mechanism that determines which user account is currently logged in and active during a web request.
    FinServ context: Compromising this filter in financial WordPress sites could allow attackers to impersonate bank administrators or access customer account management systems.
    First seen: 31 March 2026
    determine_current_user filter
    A WordPress function that decides which user account is currently active during a web request.
    FinServ context: Attackers could exploit vulnerable plugins that override this filter to impersonate bank employees or customers without proper authentication.
    First seen: 31 March 2026
    Device-Less Allow Path
    A SQL injection attack technique where attackers infer database information by measuring how long the database takes to respond to specially crafted queries.
    FinServ context: Attackers could use timing attacks against banking databases to slowly extract customer account information or transaction data without triggering obvious error messages.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    DHCP Snooping
    A security feature that monitors DHCP traffic on a network switch to prevent rogue DHCP servers and IP address conflicts.
    FinServ context: Payment processing networks use DHCP snooping to ensure only authorised devices receive IP addresses, preventing attackers from intercepting financial transactions through network spoofing.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    Digital Testing Environment
    A controlled, sandboxed technical platform where firms can build and test products or solutions without affecting live systems or real customer data.
    FinServ context: The FCA's Smart Data Accelerator uses a digital testing environment to allow firms to prototype open finance solutions safely before they influence real policy or go into production.
    First seen: 04 May 2026
    Direct Cloud VM Connections
    Cloud-native methods for logging directly into virtual machines hosted in the cloud without traditional network access.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions using cloud infrastructure need to secure these direct connection methods to prevent unauthorized access to systems processing sensitive financial data.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    DLL Hijacking
    An attack where malicious code libraries are placed in locations where legitimate applications will accidentally load them instead of the correct files.
    FinServ context: Financial software could be compromised by placing malicious DLL files in application directories, potentially allowing transaction manipulation.
    First seen: 09 April 2026
    DNSSEC Validation
    A security process that verifies the authenticity of DNS responses to ensure domain name lookups haven't been tampered with by attackers.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions performing DNSSEC validation protect against DNS poisoning attacks that could redirect customers to fraudulent banking websites during online transactions.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security)
    A security protocol that provides encrypted, tamper-resistant communication over connectionless network channels, similar to TLS but designed for protocols that do not guarantee packet delivery.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions using real-time trading or voice-over-IP systems may rely on DTLS to secure time-sensitive data transfers, making handshake parsing flaws like integer underflows a critical patching priority.
    First seen: 01 May 2026
    Dual Role Conflict (Client Money Context)
    A governance failure where the same individual or team is responsible for both making payments from a client money account and independently checking that those payments are authorised and correct.
    FinServ context: The FCA censured Sapia because staff who could initiate payments from WealthTek client money accounts were also performing the oversight checks, removing the independent safeguard required under FCA client money rules.
    First seen: 01 May 2026
    Dynamic Callable Syntax
    A programming feature that allows functions or methods to be called indirectly using variable names or expressions.
    FinServ context: Attackers can abuse dynamic callable syntax in financial applications to bypass security filters and execute unauthorized code that manipulates transaction processing.
    First seen: 08 April 2026
    Dynamic Fragment Tags
    Special HTML comments used by caching plugins to mark sections of web pages that should be processed dynamically rather than served from cache.
    FinServ context: Vulnerable dynamic fragment implementations in financial web applications can expose security tokens, potentially allowing attackers to bypass authentication on banking portals.
    First seen: 02 April 2026
    Dyre
    A banking trojan that was a predecessor to TrickBot, used to target financial institutions.
    FinServ context: Understanding Dyre helps financial services security teams trace the evolution of banking-focused malware families that specifically target their sector.
    First seen: 27 March 2026
    E
    Edge Device
    A piece of networking hardware, such as a router, VPN gateway, or firewall, that sits at the boundary between an organisation's internal network and the internet.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms are advised to baseline traffic on edge devices and monitor VPN connections, as these are frequently targeted by nation-state actors seeking persistent covert access.
    First seen: 24 April 2026
    Egg Hunter Shellcode
    A small piece of code that searches through memory to find and execute a larger payload that has been placed elsewhere.
    FinServ context: Sophisticated attacks against financial trading platforms may use egg hunter techniques to locate and execute code that manipulates market data or steals algorithmic trading secrets.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    EKANS
    A type of ransomware written in the Go programming language that encrypts files and demands payment for decryption.
    FinServ context: EKANS poses particular risk to financial services as it can disrupt critical operational systems and has been used in attacks against multiple business sectors including energy and healthcare infrastructure that banks depend on.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    End-of-Life (EoL) Device
    Hardware or software that the manufacturer no longer supports with security updates, leaving any discovered vulnerabilities permanently unpatched.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms must maintain an asset inventory that flags EoL devices, as regulators and frameworks such as DORA treat continued use of unsupported technology as an unacceptable operational risk.
    First seen: 23 April 2026
    Error-Based SQL Injection
    A technique where a single query triggers multiple additional queries, potentially causing excessive resource consumption.
    FinServ context: Malicious actors could exploit AI-powered financial advisory systems by crafting prompts that cause excessive database queries, driving up cloud costs and degrading service performance.
    First seen: 13 April 2026
    Excessive Queries
    A denial-of-service technique where an attacker sends an abnormally high volume of requests to an AI service to exhaust its resources or inflate its operating costs.
    FinServ context: A UK bank offering AI-powered customer services could face unexpected cost spikes or service outages if an adversary floods the system with excessive queries designed to drain API budgets.
    First seen: 15 April 2026
    Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP)
    A framework used in wireless networks and point-to-point connections that provides a standard way for different authentication methods to work together.
    FinServ context: EAP vulnerabilities could compromise the security of wireless payment terminals or mobile banking applications that rely on secure wireless authentication.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    Extortion Gang
    A cybercriminal group that steals data or disrupts systems and then demands payment from victims under threat of publishing stolen data or continuing the attack.
    FinServ context: The ShinyHunters extortion gang's claim of responsibility for the Instructure breach highlights the reputational and regulatory risks financial services firms face when third-party technology providers suffer data theft.
    First seen: 04 May 2026
    Extortion Group
    A cybercriminal organisation that steals sensitive data and threatens to publish or sell it unless the victim pays a ransom.
    FinServ context: The ShinyHunters extortion group breached ADT and stole data on 5.5 million people, a reminder that third-party suppliers used by financial firms can be targeted to reach large customer datasets indirectly.
    First seen: 28 April 2026
    F
    F-string Validation
    Security checks that ensure user-controlled format strings in Python code cannot execute arbitrary expressions or access sensitive variables.
    FinServ context: AI-powered financial chatbots using LangChain prompt templates need proper f-string validation to prevent attackers from injecting code that could expose customer account information.
    First seen: 11 April 2026
    FACT (Framework for Agentic Commerce Trust)
    An industry framework designed to establish rules and controls for verifying and securing financial transactions that are initiated automatically by AI agents rather than human beings.
    FinServ context: As AI agents increasingly initiate payments on behalf of customers in financial services, frameworks like FACT are needed to ensure those transactions are properly authorised and auditable.
    First seen: 25 April 2026
    FCA AI Live Testing
    A regulatory programme run by the FCA that allows selected financial firms to test AI applications in a live environment under regulatory supervision before broader deployment.
    FinServ context: Major firms including Barclays and Lloyds Banking Group are participating in the second cohort, meaning their AI models are being evaluated for consumer safety and risk management in real operational conditions.
    First seen: 28 April 2026
    FCA Censure
    A formal public statement of disapproval issued by the Financial Conduct Authority against a firm that has breached regulatory rules, stopping short of a financial penalty.
    FinServ context: Sapia received an FCA censure alongside agreeing to a voluntary £19m payment, demonstrating that censure can accompany significant financial remediation for client money failures.
    First seen: 04 May 2026
    Filesystem ACL (Access Control List)
    A set of rules attached to files or directories that defines which users or system accounts are permitted to read, write, or execute them.
    FinServ context: Overly permissive filesystem ACLs on automation directories in financial services infrastructure can allow attackers to plant malicious scripts that execute with SYSTEM-level privileges during scheduled tasks.
    First seen: 16 April 2026
    Financial Theft
    A cyber attack objective where adversaries steal money or monetary resources through various technical or social engineering methods.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms are primary targets for financial theft attacks including ransomware, cryptocurrency exploitation, and unauthorized fund transfers through compromised accounts.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Finfluencer
    A social media influencer who promotes financial products or investment opportunities to their followers, sometimes without proper authorisation or regulation.
    FinServ context: UK financial services firms must ensure any third-party social media promotions comply with FCA financial promotion rules, or they risk enforcement action similar to that taken against illegal finfluencers in the FCA's 2026 week of action.
    First seen: 24 April 2026
    Flexible PIC Concentrators (FPCs)
    Hardware components in network routers that process and forward data packets at high speeds.
    FinServ context: Compromised FPCs in financial networks could allow attackers to intercept or manipulate high-frequency trading communications and payment processing traffic.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    Frontier LLM
    A cutting-edge large language model at the leading edge of AI capability, typically developed by major AI research organisations and offering the most advanced performance currently available.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms evaluating AI adoption must assess the additional security risks posed by frontier LLMs, which may have greater capability to assist attackers in crafting sophisticated fraud or social engineering attacks.
    First seen: 04 May 2026
    G
    Gateway-Side Tool Access
    The ability to invoke tools or execute functions on the central gateway component of a networked system, rather than being restricted to actions on the local or paired node.
    FinServ context: In CVE-2026-41378, nodes with the role of "node" were incorrectly granted unrestricted gateway-side tool access, meaning a compromised credential at a peripheral node could give an attacker full control of the central gateway in a financial services platform.
    First seen: 29 April 2026
    Generative AI Models
    AI systems that can create new content like text, images, or code based on prompts and training data.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions use generative AI for customer service chatbots and document analysis, but attackers can also use these models to create sophisticated phishing emails or malicious code targeting banking systems.
    First seen: 01 April 2026
    Golang
    A programming language developed by Google that is known for its simplicity, efficiency, and strong support for concurrent programming.
    FinServ context: Financial malware like EKANS ransomware is increasingly written in Golang because it can easily target multiple operating systems and is harder for traditional security tools to analyse.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    Groovy Evaluator
    A component that executes Groovy programming language code dynamically at runtime, often used for scripting and automation.
    FinServ context: Compromised Groovy evaluators in financial workflow systems could allow attackers to inject malicious scripts that alter payment processing rules or extract sensitive customer data.
    First seen: 08 April 2026
    GroovyEvaluator
    A component in PowerJob that executes Groovy programming language code but lacks proper input validation, making it vulnerable to code injection.
    FinServ context: In financial systems using PowerJob, attackers could exploit GroovyEvaluator to execute malicious code and potentially access sensitive financial data or trading systems.
    First seen: 08 April 2026
    Guardrails (AI)
    Built-in safety controls and behavioural boundaries applied to an AI model to prevent it from producing harmful, illegal, or policy-violating outputs.
    FinServ context: A UK bank deploying a customer-facing AI assistant must configure guardrails to prevent the model from giving unauthorised financial advice or disclosing sensitive account information.
    First seen: 01 May 2026
    H
    hard-coded kill-list
    A pre-programmed list of specific software processes that malware will automatically terminate when it runs.
    FinServ context: Ransomware targeting financial services often includes kill-lists to stop security software, backup systems, or trading platforms before encrypting critical data.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Header Block Terminator
    A specific sequence of characters that signals the end of HTTP headers in web communications.
    FinServ context: Manipulating these terminators could allow attackers to smuggle malicious requests past security controls protecting financial web services.
    First seen: 28 March 2026
    Heap-based Buffer Overflow
    A vulnerability where a program writes more data to a memory buffer than it can hold, potentially allowing attackers to execute malicious code.
    FinServ context: This could compromise financial document processing systems, allowing attackers to execute code when employees open malicious client files or financial reports.
    First seen: 15 April 2026
    I
    ICS Software Platforms
    Industrial Control Systems software used to monitor and control manufacturing and operational technology environments.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms with trading floors or data centers may run ICS software that could be targeted by ransomware with specific kill-lists.
    First seen: 27 March 2026
    Impersonate
    To fraudulently assume another user's identity and permissions within a computer system.
    FinServ context: Attackers could impersonate bank employees or customers to access sensitive financial data or authorize unauthorized transactions.
    First seen: 30 March 2026
    improper access control
    A security flaw where systems fail to properly restrict what files, data, or functions users can access based on their permission levels.
    FinServ context: Banking applications with improper access control might allow junior staff to access senior executive financial reports or customer data beyond their authorization level.
    First seen: 08 April 2026
    Improper Neutralization
    A security weakness where an application fails to properly sanitize or validate user input before using it in commands, queries, or other operations.
    FinServ context: A common cause of vulnerabilities in fintech applications where customer input data isn't properly cleaned before being processed by payment systems or database queries.
    First seen: 03 April 2026
    Indirect Prompt Injection
    An attack where malicious instructions are hidden inside external content (such as a webpage or document) that an AI agent is asked to read, causing the agent to follow the attacker's instructions rather than the user's.
    FinServ context: An AI assistant used by a wealth management firm to summarise client emails could be hijacked via indirect prompt injection embedded in a malicious attachment, causing it to silently exfiltrate account details.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    Inference Engines
    Software components that process AI models to generate predictions or responses from input data.
    FinServ context: Compromised inference engines in financial AI systems could manipulate credit scoring, fraud detection, or algorithmic trading decisions.
    First seen: 28 March 2026
    Information Repositories
    Systems designed to store and share information between users, such as document libraries, wikis, or collaboration platforms.
    FinServ context: Attackers may target financial firms' information repositories like SharePoint or Confluence to steal sensitive documents containing regulatory reports or customer data.
    First seen: 23 March 2026
    Insecure Default File Permissions
    A security weakness where software creates files with access controls that are too permissive, allowing unintended users or processes to read or modify them.
    FinServ context: An AI SDK storing memory or session data with insecure file permissions in a financial services environment could expose sensitive customer interaction logs to other users on the same system.
    First seen: 16 April 2026
    Insecure Deserialization
    A vulnerability where applications unsafely convert serialized data back into objects, potentially allowing attackers to execute malicious code.
    FinServ context: Trading platforms that serialize market data or user sessions are vulnerable to attackers injecting malicious payloads that could manipulate trades or steal credentials.
    First seen: 30 March 2026
    Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
    A vulnerability where an application exposes internal object references (like database keys or file names) that attackers can manipulate to access unauthorized data.
    FinServ context: In payment systems, IDOR vulnerabilities could allow attackers to access other customers' transaction records or complete purchases they haven't paid for by manipulating order IDs.
    First seen: 30 March 2026
    Installation Credentials
    Authentication information used to set up or configure software applications and their connections to other systems.
    FinServ context: Compromised installation credentials for banking software could allow attackers to reconfigure fraud detection systems or payment gateways.
    First seen: 30 March 2026
    Integer Overflow
    A programming error that occurs when an arithmetic operation produces a result too large to be stored in the allocated memory space.
    FinServ context: Integer overflow vulnerabilities in network protocols used by financial systems can be exploited to crash trading platforms or payment processing services.
    First seen: 02 April 2026
    Integer Underflow
    A programming error that occurs when a calculation produces a number smaller than the minimum value a variable can hold, causing it to wrap around to an unexpectedly large number and potentially corrupt memory.
    FinServ context: An integer underflow in a payment processing library could cause a transaction amount or buffer size to be miscalculated, opening the door to memory corruption or denial-of-service attacks against banking infrastructure.
    First seen: 01 May 2026
    Internet Key Exchange version 2 (IKEv2)
    A protocol used to establish secure VPN connections by negotiating encryption keys and authentication methods between network devices.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions rely on IKEv2 for secure site-to-site connections between branches and data centres, making vulnerabilities in this protocol a critical concern for maintaining secure banking networks.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    IOCTL
    A pattern where a single request triggers multiple simultaneous database or API queries, often exponentially increasing system load.
    FinServ context: Attackers could exploit AI-powered financial advisory systems to generate excessive database queries, overwhelming trading platforms or customer data systems.
    First seen: 07 April 2026
    J
    JPEG driver
    A situation where a single query triggers multiple additional queries to be executed, often exponentially increasing the computational load.
    FinServ context: Attackers may exploit AI agents in financial systems to perform excessive query fan-outs, driving up cloud costs and potentially causing service disruptions during peak trading hours.
    First seen: 08 April 2026
    Juniper Networks Apstra
    A network attack where an adversary intercepts and potentially alters communications between two parties who believe they are directly communicating with each other.
    FinServ context: In financial services, these attacks could intercept trading communications or payment instructions between banks and their partners.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    Jupyter Notebooks
    Interactive coding documents widely used by data scientists to write, run, and share code, often containing configuration details and credentials.
    FinServ context: Financial services AI teams using Jupyter notebooks for model development may inadvertently expose API keys or model configurations if those notebooks are stored insecurely or published publicly.
    First seen: 15 April 2026
    Just-in-Time Access
    A security mechanism that grants users elevated permissions only when needed and for a limited time period, rather than permanent elevated access.
    FinServ context: Banks implement just-in-time access so that employees only get administrative privileges to payment systems temporarily when required for specific tasks.
    First seen: 22 March 2026
    K
    Kaggle-MCP
    A specific, pre-defined sequence of network packets that a backdoor listens for before activating, so it stays hidden until the attacker deliberately triggers it.
    FinServ context: The cd00r backdoor uses a secret knock technique, meaning it could sit undetected on a bank's UNIX server for months, only revealing itself when the attacker sends the correct traffic sequence.
    First seen: 28 April 2026
    Key Exchange without Entity Authentication
    A vulnerability where cryptographic key exchange occurs without properly verifying the identity of the communicating parties.
    FinServ context: This flaw allows attackers to intercept SSH connections between financial network management systems and devices, potentially capturing banking credentials or transaction data.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    Kill-list
    A pre-configured list of specific processes or services that malware automatically terminates when it executes on a target system.
    FinServ context: Banking trojans may use kill-lists to shut down security software or trading applications before deploying their payload.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    L
    LangChain
    An open-source framework used to build applications that connect large language models to external data sources, tools, and workflows.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms using LangChain to build AI-powered document analysis or customer service tools must patch promptly, as vulnerabilities in its prompt templates or file-loading functions can expose sensitive data.
    First seen: 16 April 2026
    Langflow
    A visual programming framework that allows users to build AI applications and workflows through a drag-and-drop interface without writing code.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms using Langflow to create customer service chatbots or document processing workflows face code injection risks that could compromise sensitive customer data.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    Large Language Models (LLMs)
    AI systems trained on vast amounts of text data that can understand and generate human-like text responses.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions using LLMs for customer service chatbots face risks from prompt injection attacks that could manipulate the AI to reveal sensitive customer account information.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    Lateral Movement
    A technique used by attackers to progressively move through a network or set of connected systems after initial access, seeking higher privileges or more valuable data.
    FinServ context: Stolen OAuth tokens are particularly dangerous in financial services cloud environments because they enable lateral movement between connected systems such as data platforms, AI tools and customer databases without triggering password-based alerts.
    First seen: 21 April 2026
    Lazarus Group
    A highly sophisticated, North Korea state-sponsored threat actor known for conducting financially motivated cyberattacks, including bank heists and cryptocurrency theft, as well as espionage operations.
    FinServ context: Lazarus has a long history of targeting financial institutions and cryptocurrency platforms, making it a direct threat to UK financial services firms and their supply chains.
    First seen: 25 April 2026
    Lifecycle Policies
    Automated rules that manage how long data is stored in cloud storage before being moved, archived, or deleted.
    FinServ context: Attackers with access to a bank's cloud storage could modify lifecycle policies to immediately delete all customer records and transaction histories.
    First seen: 22 March 2026
    Lifecycle-Triggered Deletion
    A telecommunications fraud where attackers use a victim's messaging system to send large volumes of SMS messages to premium numbers they control to generate revenue.
    FinServ context: Attackers could exploit a bank's customer verification system that sends SMS codes, flooding premium rate numbers to generate fraudulent telecom revenue while disrupting legitimate customer authentication.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    LLM Jailbreak
    A technique where an attacker tricks an AI language model into ignoring its safety rules and producing content or taking actions it was designed to prevent.
    FinServ context: A jailbroken LLM deployed in a financial services chatbot could be manipulated into revealing customer data, generating fraudulent documents, or bypassing compliance guardrails.
    First seen: 01 May 2026
    Local Privilege Escalation
    An attack where a user who already has limited access to a system exploits a vulnerability to gain administrator or root-level control without authorisation.
    FinServ context: An attacker who has compromised a low-privilege service account on a financial firm's Linux server could use a local privilege escalation flaw to access sensitive trading data or cryptographic key stores.
    First seen: 01 May 2026
    M
    Machine-in-the-Middle Attack
    Bootstrap Protocol, an older network protocol used by devices to automatically obtain their IP address and other network configuration when they start up.
    FinServ context: BOOTP vulnerabilities in financial network infrastructure could allow attackers to disrupt trading floor systems or branch office connectivity by causing network configuration chaos.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    Macros (Malicious)
    Small automated scripts embedded in documents like Word or Excel files that run code automatically when the file is opened.
    FinServ context: Threat groups have used malicious macros in email attachments to deliver backdoors targeting financial sector employees, making macro-enabled documents a key concern for FinServ security teams.
    First seen: 15 April 2026
    matlab-mcp-server
    An open-source Model Context Protocol server that exposes MATLAB code generation and execution capabilities as tools that AI agents can call remotely.
    FinServ context: CVE-2026-7272 demonstrated that a path traversal flaw in this MCP server's script execution function could be exploited remotely, posing a risk to any financial firm using AI-assisted data analysis workflows built on MATLAB.
    First seen: 29 April 2026
    MCP Server (Model Context Protocol Server)
    A server that provides tools and data sources to an AI agent, extending what the agent can do by giving it the ability to call external services or read external content.
    FinServ context: If a financial services AI assistant connects to an MCP server that scrapes external content, an attacker could embed malicious instructions in that content to hijack the agent's actions and exfiltrate credentials.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    Medusa Ransomware
    A ransomware-as-a-service strain operated by a financially motivated cybercrime group that encrypts victim data and demands payment, often targeting organisations at high speed using known vulnerabilities.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms are prime targets for Medusa campaigns because of their ability to pay ransoms and the operational impact of encrypted transaction or customer data systems.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    MegaCortex
    A ransomware variant that uses hardcoded lists of processes to terminate before encrypting systems.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions need to understand ransomware variants like MegaCortex that may specifically target trading or banking applications.
    First seen: 27 March 2026
    Memory Corruption
    A programming error where a program incorrectly modifies memory locations, potentially allowing attackers to control program execution.
    FinServ context: Memory corruption in high-frequency trading systems could be exploited to manipulate trade orders or leak proprietary trading strategies to competitors.
    First seen: 07 April 2026
    memory exhaustion
    An attack technique that forces a system to use up all available memory, causing applications to crash or become unresponsive.
    FinServ context: Memory exhaustion attacks against financial trading systems could disrupt market operations or prevent customers from accessing their accounts.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Memory Leak
    A software bug where a program gradually uses up more and more memory over time without properly releasing it back to the system.
    FinServ context: Memory leaks in financial trading systems or payment processors can cause critical services to slow down or crash during peak transaction periods, potentially resulting in significant financial losses.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    MetaGPT
    An AI framework that assigns different roles to GPT models to collaborate on complex tasks like software development and project management.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions might use MetaGPT-like tools for automated report generation or regulatory compliance documentation, making command injection vulnerabilities particularly dangerous.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    Microsoft Copilot
    An AI assistant integrated into Microsoft 365 products that can read, summarise, and act on documents, emails, and data within an organisation's environment.
    FinServ context: At a financial services firm, a compromised or manipulated Copilot instance could be used to silently exfiltrate trade data, customer records, or internal reports through prompt injection attacks.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    Mirai Botnet
    A type of malware that infects internet-connected devices, particularly routers and IoT devices, and recruits them into a network used to launch large-scale attacks.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms with internet-exposed network devices must ensure end-of-life hardware is replaced promptly, as Mirai campaigns actively target unpatched routers to build attack infrastructure that can be directed at financial sector targets.
    First seen: 23 April 2026
    MITM Attacker
    An adversary who positions themselves between two communicating parties to intercept, monitor, or alter their communications.
    FinServ context: In financial services, MITM attackers can intercept payment instructions or manipulate trading data flowing between banking applications and servers.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    Model Context Protocol
    A standardized way for AI agents to access and interact with external tools and data sources.
    FinServ context: Financial AI systems using Model Context Protocol could be compromised to give attackers access to trading systems, customer databases, or risk management tools.
    First seen: 25 March 2026
    Model Weights
    The internal numerical parameters of a machine learning model that are produced during training and define how the model processes inputs and generates outputs.
    FinServ context: An attacker who gains access to a financial firm's proprietary AI model weights could reverse-engineer the model's decision logic, exposing credit scoring algorithms or fraud detection strategies to manipulation.
    First seen: 01 May 2026
    Motor Finance Redress Scheme
    A formal industry-wide compensation programme mandated by a regulator to reimburse customers who suffered financial harm due to undisclosed discretionary commission arrangements on car finance products.
    FinServ context: The FCA's motor finance redress scheme requires affected firms to implement robust customer identification and payment processes, creating significant operational and data management challenges for compliance
    Multipart Upload Handler
    A server-side component that receives files sent in multiple separate chunks over HTTP, reassembling them into a complete file on the server.
    FinServ context: Poorly validated multipart upload handlers in financial portals (for example, document submission for mortgage applications) are a common entry point for path traversal and remote code execution attacks.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    N
    n-day Vulnerability
    A security flaw that has already been publicly disclosed and has a patch available, but which attackers continue to exploit against systems that have not yet applied the fix.
    FinServ context: Storm-1175's ransomware campaigns exploiting n-day vulnerabilities highlight that UK financial institutions with slow patching cycles remain exposed even after a vulnerability is publicly known and fixed.
    First seen: 18 April 2026
    NIST NVD (National Vulnerability Database)
    A US government database maintained by NIST that catalogues known software vulnerabilities, their severity scores, and remediation guidance, built on top of the CVE list.
    FinServ context: Security engineers at UK financial firms use the NVD alongside CVE data to assess patch priority, particularly when NIST changes its scoring methodology to focus on the highest-impact vulnerabilities.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    Non-Recursive String Replace
    A sanitization method that only removes dangerous characters once, allowing attackers to bypass security by using nested malicious patterns.
    FinServ context: Financial web applications using non-recursive sanitization could allow attackers to access sensitive customer data or payment information through crafted input sequences.
    First seen: 14 April 2026
    Nonce Validation
    A security mechanism that checks whether a randomly generated number used once (nonce) is valid to prevent replay attacks.
    FinServ context: Missing nonce validation in financial web applications can allow attackers to forge requests that trigger unauthorized transactions or modify payment settings.
    First seen: 08 April 2026
    O
    OAuth Sprawl
    The accumulation of too many third-party applications connected to your systems via OAuth tokens, creating excessive and hard-to-monitor access points that increase your attack surface.
    FinServ context: A financial services firm using dozens of SaaS integrations may grant OAuth access to each one, and if any single integration is compromised, attackers can pivot into core banking or data systems.
    First seen: 30 April 2026
    OAuth Token
    A small digital key issued by an authentication system that proves a user or application has been granted permission to access a resource, without needing to share a password.
    FinServ context: In financial services, stolen OAuth tokens can allow attackers to move laterally between cloud services and AI development tools, as seen in the Vercel breach where an employee's AI tool access was compromised.
    First seen: 21 April 2026
    Ollama
    An open-source framework that allows users to run large language models locally on their own infrastructure.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms may need to monitor for unauthorized local AI model deployments that could bypass corporate AI governance policies.
    First seen: 27 March 2026
    Open Finance
    A system that allows consumers and businesses to securely share their financial data with authorised third-party providers across a wide range of financial products.
    FinServ context: UK financial services firms must prepare for FCA-regulated open finance frameworks that will require secure data-sharing APIs covering mortgages, pensions, savings, and investments.
    First seen: 18 April 2026
    Open Source Intelligence (OSINT)
    The practice of collecting and analyzing publicly available information from sources like websites, social media, and databases for security purposes.
    FinServ context: Banks use OSINT to identify threat actors researching their systems or to discover if their AI models or customer data have been exposed online.
    First seen: 30 March 2026
    OpenClaw
    A cloud-based platform or application framework that manages agents, plugins, and sandboxed environments with scope-based access controls.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms using OpenClaw for automated trading agents or compliance monitoring would need to patch privilege escalation vulnerabilities that could allow unauthorized access to sensitive financial data.
    First seen: 11 April 2026
    Oracle Financial Services Analytical Applications Infrastructure (OFSAAI)
    A software platform made by Oracle that financial services companies use to store, analyse, and report on large volumes of financial data across multiple business functions.
    FinServ context: Three separate CVEs (CVSS 7.5) were disclosed against OFSAAI, meaning unauthenticated attackers could remotely access all data held within the platform — a critical risk for any bank or insurer running it.
    First seen: 22 April 2026
    Oracle Financial Services Customer Screening
    An Oracle software product used by financial institutions to screen customers against watchlists, sanctions lists, and other regulatory checks during onboarding or transactions.
    FinServ context: A CVSS 7.5 vulnerability in the User Interface component allowed unauthenticated remote attackers to read all customer screening data, which could expose personally identifiable information and sanctions-related records.
    First seen: 22 April 2026
    Oracle Financial Services Transaction Filtering
    An Oracle software product that automatically checks financial transactions against regulatory rules, sanctions lists, and internal policies to block prohibited payments.
    FinServ context: A CVSS 7.5 flaw in the User Interface allowed unauthenticated network access via HTTP, meaning an attacker could read transaction filtering data without any credentials — a serious compliance and data-protection risk.
    First seen: 22 April 2026
    OS Command Injection
    A vulnerability where an attacker can execute operating system commands on a server by manipulating input that gets passed to system command functions.
    FinServ context: In financial services, OS command injection could allow attackers to execute commands on trading systems or payment processors, potentially accessing sensitive financial data or disrupting critical operations.
    First seen: 07 April 2026
    Out-of-Band (OOB) Security Update
    An emergency software patch released outside a vendor's normal scheduled update cycle to fix a critical vulnerability that cannot wait for the next planned release.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms running ASP.NET Core applications must apply OOB patches immediately, as delayed patching of privilege escalation flaws could breach FCA operational resilience requirements.
    First seen: 23 April 2026
    Out-of-Bounds Read
    A memory access vulnerability where a program reads data from memory locations beyond the allocated buffer boundaries.
    FinServ context: Out-of-bounds read attacks against financial network infrastructure can lead to service disruption and potential disclosure of sensitive transaction data.
    First seen: 02 April 2026
    Out-of-Memory (OOM) Conditions
    A system state where available memory is exhausted, typically causing applications to crash or become unresponsive.
    FinServ context: High-frequency trading platforms could be targeted with large payload attacks designed to trigger OOM conditions and disrupt market operations.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Output Escaping
    When a single request triggers multiple additional database or API queries, potentially creating a cascading effect.
    FinServ context: AI-powered trading systems can be manipulated to perform excessive query fan-outs that overwhelm market data APIs and exhaust trading platform budgets.
    First seen: 17 April 2026
    P
    Packet Sequence Authentication
    The practice of injecting malicious content into systems that accept public input, with the intent of affecting downstream AI processing.
    FinServ context: Criminals might insert malicious prompts into loan application forms that later poison the AI models used for credit risk assessment at banks.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    packet-capturing program
    Software that intercepts and records network traffic passing through a system for monitoring or analysis purposes.
    FinServ context: Attackers use packet-capturing backdoors in financial networks to secretly monitor sensitive communications like transaction data or customer information.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Paired Node Credentials
    Authentication credentials issued to a specific networked node that has been formally registered and trusted by a gateway or central system, allowing it to send authorised requests.
    FinServ context: CVE-2026-41378 showed that attackers holding paired node credentials in OpenClaw could abuse unrestricted agent request dispatch to escalate privileges and achieve remote code execution on the gateway — a serious risk in any financial platform using distributed agent architectures.
    First seen: 29 April 2026
    Pairing Requirements
    Authentication mechanisms that require two systems or components to establish a secure connection through a mutual verification process.
    FinServ context: Payment processing systems use pairing requirements to ensure only authorized terminals can connect to the backend, preventing rogue devices from processing fraudulent transactions.
    First seen: 11 April 2026
    Passkeys
    A login method that replaces passwords by using a cryptographic key pair stored on your device, so you authenticate using biometrics or a PIN instead of a typed password.
    FinServ context: UK financial services firms are increasingly adopting passkeys as a consumer authentication option to reduce account takeover risk and meet FCA expectations around robust customer identity verification.
    First seen: 23 April 2026
    Path Traversal Sanitization
    The process of cleaning and validating file paths to prevent attackers from accessing files outside of intended directories using sequences like "../".
    FinServ context: Banking applications must implement proper path traversal sanitization to prevent attackers from reading sensitive customer data files or configuration files containing database credentials.
    First seen: 17 April 2026
    Path Traversal Sequences
    Special character combinations like "../" that allow attackers to navigate outside intended directories to access restricted files on a server.
    FinServ context: Payment processing systems with inadequate input validation could expose sensitive transaction logs or configuration files through path traversal attacks.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Payment Amount Bypass
    A vulnerability where attackers can manipulate payment values by controlling parameters that the system uses to validate transaction amounts.
    FinServ context: This could allow fraudsters to process transactions for amounts lower than intended in banking applications or payment processing systems.
    First seen: 28 March 2026
    Payment Callback Forgery
    A technique where an attacker sends fake payment confirmation messages to a system, tricking it into believing a transaction was successful without money actually changing hands.
    FinServ context: In financial services, a forged payment callback could allow a fraudster to complete a checkout and receive goods or services without making a real payment, directly impacting revenue and fraud loss metrics.
    First seen: 16 April 2026
    PayPal Transaction Token
    A unique identifier generated by PayPal to track and validate specific payment transactions between merchants and customers.
    FinServ context: Financial applications must properly validate these tokens to prevent attackers from using a low-value payment token to complete high-value transactions fraudulently.
    First seen: 30 March 2026
    Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Crypto Trading
    The direct buying and selling of cryptocurrency between individuals without using a regulated centralised exchange as an intermediary.
    FinServ context: The FCA's first enforcement operation against illegal P2P crypto trading highlights that operating unregistered P2P crypto services in the UK is a criminal offence under the Money Laundering Regulations.
    First seen: 23 April 2026
    Permissive Cross-Domain Policy
    A misconfiguration in a web application that allows scripts from any external website to make requests to that application, potentially exposing sensitive data to unauthorised parties.
    FinServ context: In financial services API environments, a permissive cross-domain policy on a token endpoint could allow an attacker's website to silently harvest authentication tokens from a customer's browser session.
    First seen: 21 April 2026
    Pig Butchering
    A type of fraud where scammers build long-term romantic or investment relationships with victims before convincing them to make increasingly large financial investments in fake schemes.
    FinServ context: Financial firms must monitor for customers being manipulated into transferring large sums to cryptocurrency platforms or investment accounts as part of pig butchering schemes.
    First seen: 22 March 2026
    Pikabot
    A backdoor malware that provides attackers with persistent remote access to infected systems and is used to deploy additional malicious tools.
    FinServ context: Pikabot is frequently used as an initial access method in financial services attacks, allowing criminals to establish a foothold before deploying tools like Cobalt Strike for lateral movement through banking networks.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)
    Encryption algorithms designed to remain secure against attacks from quantum computers, which can break most encryption methods in widespread use today.
    FinServ context: UK financial institutions processing long-lived data such as pension records or mortgage contracts need to begin migrating to post-quantum cryptography now, because data harvested today by adversaries could be decrypted once quantum computers mature — a threat sometimes called "harvest now, decrypt later."
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    PowerDuke
    A backdoor malware that is typically delivered through malicious Microsoft Office documents containing embedded macros.
    FinServ context: PowerDuke represents a common attack vector against financial services employees who regularly receive and open Office documents from clients, suppliers, and business partners.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Principal-Appointed Representative Relationship
    A regulatory arrangement where a regulated firm (the principal) takes legal responsibility for the activities of another firm (the appointed representative) that acts on its behalf.
    FinServ context: In the Sapia/WealthTek case, Sapia's responsibility for client money extended to WealthTek's activities because it had appointed WealthTek as its representative, meaning failures by the representative exposed the principal to regulatory censure.
    First seen: 04 May 2026
    Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs)
    A group of tools and methods that allow organisations to process or share data while protecting personal information, for example by anonymising or encrypting it so individuals cannot be identified.
    FinServ context: Ant International's Alipay+ upgrade uses PETs to protect customer payment data during transactions, a model increasingly relevant to UK financial firms handling sensitive customer data under GDPR.
    First seen: 25 April 2026
    Privilege Escalation
    A security vulnerability that allows a user to gain higher access rights or permissions than they should normally have.
    FinServ context: In financial services, privilege escalation could allow a regular banking application user to gain administrative access to customer accounts or sensitive financial data.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Privilege Escalation (Zero-Day Exploitation)
    An attack where an adversary exploits an unknown, unpatched software flaw to gain higher-level system permissions than they are supposed to have before the vendor has issued
    Promiscuous Mode
    A network interface configuration that allows a device to capture and inspect all network traffic, not just traffic intended for that device.
    FinServ context: Attackers may use promiscuous mode on compromised systems in financial networks to intercept sensitive data like trading information or customer credentials passing over the network.
    First seen: 23 March 2026
    Prompt Infiltration
    A technique where malicious instructions are inserted into data sources with the intent that they will later be processed by an AI system.
    FinServ context: Attackers could plant malicious prompts in customer service databases that later cause AI chatbots to leak sensitive financial information or perform unauthorized transactions.
    First seen: 26 March 2026
    Prompt Injection
    An attack where malicious instructions are inserted into AI system prompts to manipulate the AI's behavior or extract sensitive information.
    FinServ context: Attackers could use prompt injection against a bank's AI customer service bot to trick it into revealing other customers' account details or bypassing security protocols.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Prompt Injection (in Agentic AI)
    An attack where malicious instructions hidden in content that an AI agent reads are interpreted as legitimate commands, causing the agent to take harmful or unintended actions.
    FinServ context: Prompt injection vulnerabilities in tools like Salesforce Agentforce and Microsoft Copilot could allow attackers to leak sensitive customer financial data processed by those AI systems.
    First seen: 27 April 2026
    Prompt Template
    A pre-defined text structure used to format instructions or inputs that are passed into an AI language model.
    FinServ context: In a financial services AI assistant, a poorly validated prompt template could be manipulated by an attacker to extract confidential customer data or bypass intended model behaviour.
    First seen: 16 April 2026
    Proof-of-Concept (PoC) Exploit
    A working demonstration, usually created by a researcher, that shows a vulnerability is real and exploitable, without necessarily causing harm, in order to prove the risk exists.
    FinServ context: The Backslash team published a PoC showing how an MCP server could be weaponised for indirect prompt injection against Cursor, giving financial security teams concrete evidence of the attack path to assess in their own AI development environments.
    First seen: 29 April 2026
    Proxy Delivery Mode
    A file serving method where a server acts as an intermediary, loading requested content into memory before forwarding it to the client.
    FinServ context: Financial document management systems using proxy delivery could be vulnerable to memory exhaustion attacks if attackers request large files with unbounded range headers.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA)
    The UK regulatory body, part of the Bank of England, responsible for the prudential supervision of banks, insurers, and major investment firms to ensure their financial safety and soundness.
    FinServ context: The PRA worked alongside the FCA to reform the SM&CR, aiming to reduce compliance costs for regulated financial services firms while maintaining senior manager accountability.
    First seen: 29 April 2026
    public-facing application
    Any software system that accepts input directly from internet users without requiring them to authenticate first.
    FinServ context: Bank websites, mobile apps, and customer portals are public-facing applications that could be targeted with malicious prompts intended to poison AI systems that later process the data.
    First seen: 31 March 2026
    Public-facing application infiltration
    The practice of injecting malicious prompts into publicly accessible applications with the intent that AI systems will process them later.
    FinServ context: Attackers might inject malicious prompts into a bank's customer feedback system, which could later be processed by AI tools used by support staff, potentially compromising internal systems.
    PyInterpreter
    A software component that executes Python code dynamically at runtime, often used in applications that need to run user-provided scripts.
    FinServ context: Vulnerabilities in PyInterpreter components within financial AI systems could allow attackers to execute malicious code and manipulate trading algorithms or customer data processing.
    First seen: 06 April 2026
    PyTorch / TensorFlow / JAX
    Open-source software libraries (frameworks) used by developers to build and train machine learning models, providing pre-built mathematical building blocks so engineers do not have to write everything from scratch.
    FinServ context: A bank using these frameworks to build fraud-detection models must patch them promptly, since a compromised deep learning framework could corrupt model outputs or exfiltrate training data containing customer transactions.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    Q
    Qualifying Stablecoin
    A type of cryptocurrency whose value is pegged to a stable asset (such as a fiat currency) and which falls within a specific legal definition that brings it under regulatory oversight.
    FinServ context: From October 2027, UK firms issuing or dealing in qualifying stablecoins will need FCA authorisation, creating new compliance and security obligations around cryptoasset custody and issuance.
    First seen: 18 April 2026
    Quantum-Resilience-as-a-Service (QRaaS)
    A cloud-delivered security service that upgrades a financial institution's encryption to withstand attacks from quantum computers without requiring a full replacement of existing systems.
    FinServ context: UK banks and payment networks face a future threat from quantum computing that could break current encryption standards, making QRaaS a practical option for strengthening cryptographic defences incrementally.
    First seen: 18 April 2026
    Query Fan-out
    When a single request triggers multiple additional queries or API calls to different services or databases.
    FinServ context: In banking systems, attackers might craft prompts that cause an AI agent to perform excessive database lookups across multiple account systems, overwhelming the infrastructure.
    First seen: 01 April 2026
    R
    Race Condition
    A security flaw that occurs when multiple processes access shared resources simultaneously without proper coordination, creating unpredictable results.
    FinServ context: In banking systems, race conditions could allow attackers to manipulate transaction processing timing to transfer funds multiple times or bypass account balance checks.
    First seen: 15 April 2026
    Range header
    An HTTP request component that specifies which portion of a file or data the client wants to download.
    FinServ context: Malicious Range headers can be used to exhaust memory in financial applications that serve large files like transaction reports or audit logs.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Ransomware Negotiator
    A supervised programme where AI systems are tested in real-world conditions with live data and users, under regulatory oversight, before or during broader deployment.
    FinServ context: The FCA's AI Live Testing initiative allows firms such as Barclays and Lloyds to identify safety and bias risks in production AI systems, helping demonstrate to the regulator that AI deployment meets consumer protection standards.
    First seen: 23 April 2026
    Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS)
    A business model where ransomware developers rent or licence their malicious software to other criminals, who carry out attacks and share the ransom proceeds with the developers.
    FinServ context: The RaaS model lowers the technical barrier to attacking financial institutions, meaning even non-technical criminals can deploy sophisticated ransomware like ALPHV against banks and payment processors.
    First seen: 21 April 2026
    RCE (Remote Code Execution)
    A vulnerability that allows an attacker to run arbitrary code on a target system from a remote location without physical access.
    FinServ context: Represents a critical threat to financial institutions as it can lead to complete system compromise, allowing attackers to manipulate transactions or steal sensitive financial data.
    First seen: 03 April 2026
    Redress Scheme
    A formal regulatory programme requiring firms to identify affected customers, calculate fair compensation, and make payments for a specific category of past consumer harm.
    FinServ context: Engineers at financial services firms must understand redress schemes because they often require building or auditing data pipelines that identify eligible customers and calculate compensation at scale.
    First seen: 18 April 2026
    Ref Parameter
    A Python code execution component that allows applications to run Python scripts dynamically within larger systems.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions using AI tools with PyInterpreter functionality face code injection risks where malicious Python code could access sensitive customer data or trading systems.
    First seen: 06 April 2026
    Referral Parser
    A software component that processes and analyzes information about where web traffic originated from.
    FinServ context: Financial services websites use referral parsers to understand customer acquisition channels, but poorly designed parsers can become injection points for malicious code.
    First seen: 17 April 2026
    Regex-Based Validation
    A security control that uses regular expressions to check if user input matches expected patterns before processing.
    FinServ context: Poorly implemented regex validation in financial APIs can be bypassed by attackers to execute commands that manipulate payment processing or account data.
    First seen: 02 April 2026
    Remote Access Trojan (RAT)
    A category of malicious software that secretly gives an attacker full remote control of an infected device, including the ability to view files, execute commands, and monitor activity.
    FinServ context: RATs targeting financial services employees could allow attackers to silently monitor trading terminals, extract client data, or initiate fraudulent transactions.
    First seen: 27 April 2026
    Request Smuggling
    An attack technique where differences in how web servers and proxies parse HTTP requests allow attackers to bypass security controls.
    FinServ context: This could enable attackers to bypass fraud detection systems or access controls protecting financial APIs and services.
    First seen: 28 March 2026
    Resource Hijacking
    An attack where a threat actor secretly uses a victim organisation's computing infrastructure — such as cloud servers or GPUs — for the attacker's own purposes, typically cryptocurrency mining or running AI workloads.
    FinServ context: A bank running large AI inference workloads in the cloud is an attractive target for resource hijacking, as the high baseline of compute usage can mask unauthorised usage and inflate cloud bills before detection.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    Resource-Intensive Queries
    A specific sequence of network traffic or data packets that triggers a backdoor or hidden functionality when detected.
    FinServ context: Malicious actors could embed secret knock sequences in financial transaction data to activate dormant backdoors in payment processing systems without triggering standard security monitoring.
    First seen: 06 April 2026
    REST API Endpoint
    A specific URL path in a web service that accepts HTTP requests and returns data in a standardized format.
    FinServ context: Unsecured REST API endpoints in financial management systems can expose customer account information or transaction histories to unauthorized users.
    First seen: 02 April 2026
    Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
    An AI technique that combines a large language model with a searchable database to provide more accurate and up-to-date responses.
    FinServ context: Financial institutions use RAG systems to help customer service chatbots access current account information and regulatory documents when responding to client queries.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Retrieval Content Crafting
    A Chinese state-sponsored advanced persistent threat group known for conducting both espionage and financially motivated attacks, targeting cloud infrastructure and software supply chains globally.
    FinServ context: APT41's targeting of major cloud platforms such as AWS and Azure using typosquatting to hide command-and-control traffic poses a direct risk to financial firms relying on cloud infrastructure for core banking and data services.
    First seen: 25 April 2026
    RLIKE-Based Boolean SQL Injection
    A specific SQL injection technique that uses the RLIKE operator to create true/false conditions for extracting database information character by character.
    FinServ context: This attack method could be used against financial applications to slowly extract customer account details, transaction histories, or authentication credentials from banking databases.
    First seen: 06 April 2026
    RMM Tool (Remote Monitoring and Management)
    Software used by IT teams or managed service providers to remotely monitor, manage, and control systems across an organisation's infrastructure.
    FinServ context: A critical RCE vulnerability in Bomgar RMM illustrates how attackers can exploit trusted IT management tools already present in financial services environments to spread ransomware across connected systems.
    First seen: 04 May 2026
    Rolling Release (Software)
    A software development model where updates are continuously published without fixed version numbers, making it harder to pinpoint exactly which version contains a vulnerability.
    FinServ context: Several MCP server CVEs affecting financial AI tooling were published with no specific affected version listed because the projects use rolling releases, complicating patch management for security teams.
    First seen: 28 April 2026
    ROP Chain
    Return-Oriented Programming chain is an exploitation technique that chains together small code snippets (gadgets) already present in memory to execute malicious operations.
    FinServ context: ROP chains can bypass modern security protections in banking applications to execute unauthorized transactions or access sensitive customer data.
    First seen: 13 April 2026
    Roundcube Webmail Exploitation
    A technique where attackers exploit security flaws in the open-source Roundcube email platform to run malicious code on a victim's system simply by having them open an email.
    FinServ context: Financial services staff using webmail platforms face similar zero-interaction risks, where opening a legitimate-looking email could trigger code execution without any further user action.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    S
    Safety Alignment
    The process of training an AI model so that its outputs and behaviour remain consistent with intended human values, policies, and ethical boundaries.
    FinServ context: Poor safety alignment in a financial services AI model could allow it to recommend unsuitable investment products or circumvent regulatory obligations such as FCA conduct rules.
    First seen: 01 May 2026
    Salesforce Agentforce
    A platform from Salesforce that allows organisations to build and deploy AI agents capable of performing tasks and interacting with business data on behalf of users.
    FinServ context: Financial services firms deploying Agentforce to handle customer queries or process data must ensure prompt injection vulnerabilities are patched to prevent attackers leaking sensitive client information.
    First seen: 20 April 2026
    Sandbox Escape
    A security breach where malicious code breaks out of a restricted execution environment designed to isolate it from the host system.
    FinServ context: Sandbox escape vulnerabilities in financial trading platforms could allow attackers to access real market data or execute unauthorized trades.
    First seen: 02 April 2026
    Sandbox Roots
    A programming error where data written to a memory buffer exceeds the buffer's boundaries and overwrites adjacent memory locations on the program's call stack.
    FinServ context: Stack buffer overflows in financial trading applications could allow attackers to manipulate transaction data or bypass authentication controls by overwriting return addresses.
    First seen: 13 April 2026
    Sanitization Issue
    A software vulnerability where input or output data is not properly cleaned or validated, allowing an attacker to sneak in malicious content that the application then processes as legitimate instructions.
    FinServ context: A sanitization issue in an AI-based filesystem tool allowed prompt injection to escape the sandbox, illustrating how poor input validation in AI products can lead to full system compromise in a financial services environment.
    First seen: 25 April 2026
    Satellite data files
    Data files containing information transmitted from or to satellite communication systems.
    FinServ context: Financial firms using satellite communications for remote trading offices or disaster recovery sites may process satellite data files that could contain malicious payloads.
    First seen: 07 April 2026
    Scattered Spider
    A loosely organised cybercriminal collective known for using social engineering and SIM-swapping techniques to compromise large organisations and steal cryptocurrency.
    FinServ context: Scattered Spider's tactics of impersonating IT helpdesk staff pose a direct threat to financial services firms, where employees may be manipulated into resetting credentials or bypassing security controls.
    First seen: 21 April 2026
    Scope Boundary Bypass
    A security flaw where an application incorrectly grants higher-level permissions than what was originally authorized to a user or process.
    FinServ context: In banking APIs, scope boundary bypass could allow a read-only trading application to execute unauthorized transactions by gaining admin-level API access.
    First seen: 11 April 2026
    Scope Validation
    The security process of checking whether a user or system has the appropriate permissions to access specific resources or perform certain actions.
    FinServ context: In financial systems, insufficient scope validation could allow junior staff to approve high-value transactions or access sensitive customer data beyond their authorization level.
    First seen: 01 April 2026
    Scope-Upgrade Requests
    Requests to expand the permissions or access level of an authenticated session or device beyond its initially granted privileges.
    FinServ context: In banking systems, unauthorized scope upgrades could allow attackers to escalate from read-only access to administrative control over payment processing systems.
    First seen: 10 April 2026
    Secondary Markets
    Financial markets where investors buy and sell previously issued securities such as shares, bonds, and derivatives, rather than buying directly from the issuer.
    FinServ context: The FCA's advisory committee on secondary markets helps shape regulation of equities, derivatives, and fixed income trading to protect market integrity in UK financial services.
    First seen: 04 May 2026
    Secret Knock
    A specific sequence of network packets or traffic patterns that triggers dormant malware to activate and execute its payload.
    FinServ context: Advanced persistent threats in financial networks may use secret knocks to remain undetected until receiving activation signals from command and control servers.
    First seen: 24 March 2026
    Security Metrics
    Measurable values used to assess how well an organisation's security controls, processes, and teams are performing over time.
    FinServ context: Poorly chosen security metrics in a financial services SOC can create a false sense of security, masking genuine exposure to fraud, data breaches, or regulatory breaches.
    First seen: 27 April 2026
    Security Metrics (SOC)
    Quantitative measures used to assess the effectiveness and performance of a security operations centre, such as mean time to detect or number of incidents resolved.
    FinServ context: Choosing the wrong SOC metrics — for example, measuring ticket volume rather than detection quality — can give a false sense of security in a financial firm and mask genuine gaps in threat coverage.
    First seen: 28 April 2026
    Segregation of Duties
    A control principle that requires different people to be responsible for different parts of a sensitive process, so no single person can carry out and conceal fraud or errors alone.
    FinServ context: The FCA's censure of Sapia highlights that allowing the same staff to authorise and audit client money payments violates segregation of duties, a fundamental control required under FCA CASS rules.
    First seen: 24 April 2026
    Segregation of Duties (Client Money Context)
    The practice of separating financial responsibilities so that no single person can both authorise and verify the same transaction or account activity.
    FinServ context: The FCA found Sapia failed to segregate duties properly — staff who could move money from client accounts were also the ones checking those accounts, increasing fraud risk.
    First seen: 28 April 2026
    Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SM&CR)
    A UK regulatory framework that assigns clear personal accountability to senior individuals at financial services firms for the areas of the business they manage.
    FinServ context: UK financial firms must ensure that senior managers are formally approved and that their responsibilities are documented, meaning a security incident could result in personal regulatory liability for named individuals.
    First seen: 25 April 2026
    Serialized Payload
    Malicious data that has been converted into a format for storage or transmission but contains hidden code that executes when processed.
    FinServ context: Banking APIs receiving serialized transaction data could be exploited if the payload contains code designed to manipulate account balances or transfer funds.
    First seen: 30 March 2026
    Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
    A vulnerability that tricks a server into making HTTP requests to internal systems or external URLs that the attacker specifies, rather than the server choosing what to connect to itself.
